planting.note: Plant rhizomes horizontally 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; in zones 8–9 mulch rhizomes heavily in fall to protect from frost. Shampoo ginger is one of the most shade-tolerant of the Zingiber species, producing dramatic cone-like inflorescences that turn brilliant red as they mature and exude a slippery, soapy fluid used traditionally as a hair conditioner in tropical Asia.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally 2–3 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; in zone 8–9 mulch rhizomes heavily in fall to protect from frost. Zingiber zerumbet is a tall, dramatic tropical ginger grown both for its bold ornamental foliage and its unusual cone-like inflorescences — initially green and cream-banded, turning brilliant red as they mature — that produce a slippery, soapy fluid traditionally used as a hair conditioner and shampoo in tropical Asia; the plant is widely used in SE Asian cooking and traditional medicine. It grows naturally in moist, shaded tropical forests and is one of the most shade-tolerant of the Zingiber species; in zone 9–10 gardens it makes a spectacular background plant for shaded tropical borders, producing dramatic red cone heads even in quite deep shade. The rhizomes have a pungent, camphor-like scent and some medicinal uses in Ayurveda.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes horizontally 1–2 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; kaempferia galanga is a ground-hugging, no-stem species with leaves that lie nearly flat on the soil and is one of the most shade-tolerant of all culinary gingers. It can be grown in containers and brought indoors in zones below 9.

directions: Plant rhizomes horizontally 1–2 inches deep in spring in moist, humus-rich, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; kaempferia galanga is a small, ground-hugging tropical herb with flat, round, beautifully marked leaves and white flowers with a violet center that bloom at soil level; the aromatic rhizomes are a key flavoring ingredient in Indonesian, Thai, and Malaysian cuisine (known as kencur, cekur, or pro hom) and have a distinctive, camphor-pine-earthy aroma quite unlike other galangals. It can be grown in containers and brought indoors in zones below 9; in zone 9–10 it thrives in partial to deep shade under tropical canopy and goes dormant in winter.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant small rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in spring after soil warms to 70°F in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; dancing ladies ginger dies back to the ground each winter in zones 8–9 but reliably re-emerges from the rhizome in spring. Excellent in containers, shade gardens, and as a companion to begonias, caladiums, and ferns.

directions: Plant small rhizomes 1–2 inches deep in spring after soil warms to 70°F in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; Globba winitii is a charming, small Thai ginger producing arching, pendant inflorescences with decorative pink to purple bracts and tiny, vivid yellow flowers that dangle in a way that resembles a line of dancing figures, earning its delightful common name; it is one of the finest small gingers for shaded tropical and subtropical gardens. In late summer and fall the bracts are replaced by tiny bulbils that drop to the ground and produce new plants, gradually naturalizing the planting. Dancing ladies ginger dies back to the ground each winter in zones 8–9 but reliably re-emerges from the rhizome in spring. Excellent in containers, shade gardens, and as a companion to begonias, caladiums, and ferns.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Costus arabicus tolerates brief drought better than some gingers once established but prefers consistently moist conditions and partial shade to prevent leaf scorch. One of the most shade-tolerant of the spiral gingers, making an attractive medium-sized specimen for partially shaded tropical-style gardens in zones 9–11.

directions: Plant rhizomes in spring in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; Costus arabicus (syn. Costus spicatus in some references) is a tropical Americas spiral ginger with the characteristic spiraling leaf arrangement of the Costus genus and attractive cone-like terminal flower heads producing white, orchid-like flowers with orange centers from summer through fall; it is one of the most shade-tolerant of the spiral gingers and makes an attractive, medium-sized specimen for partially shaded tropical-style gardens in zones 9–11. The rhizomes are used in traditional Caribbean and South American folk medicine as a diuretic and anti-inflammatory remedy. Tolerates brief drought better than some gingers once established but prefers consistently moist conditions.

planting.note: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; provide a trellis or other structure for the vining stems to climb. As a legume it fixes atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility while producing food. Thrives in moist, partially shaded woodland edges, streambanks, and open forest understories.

directions: Plant tubers 3–4 inches deep in spring in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, loamy to sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; provide a trellis or other structure for the vining stems to climb. Apios americana is a native North American twining vine in the legume family that produces chains of small, starchy, potato-like tubers in the soil that were a major food staple for dozens of indigenous tribes throughout eastern North America and reportedly sustained the Pilgrims through their first harsh New England winter; the tubers can be boiled, roasted, or mashed like potatoes with a sweeter, nuttier flavor. As a legume, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility while producing food. The vine also produces attractive, fragrant, dark-rose to reddish-brown flower clusters in late summer that are excellent for native bees; it thrives in moist, partially shaded woodland edges, streambanks, and open forest understories.
